column1: Below are comprehensive tables of all the major numbered lists found across the Lotus Sutra, based on the provided search results and established scholarship. These lists are central to the structure and doctrine of the sutra, appearing as parables, enumerations of beings, virtues, and doctrinal categories.

column3: Summary/Description

A wealthy man’s house catches fire while his many children are inside, absorbed in play and unaware of the danger. To lure them out, the father promises them three kinds of carts (goat, deer, ox). When they escape, he gives each a magnificent cart drawn by a white ox, far surpassing what was promised. The burning house symbolizes the world (samsara), the father is the Buddha, and the children are sentient beings. The carts represent different Buddhist paths, and the great cart is the One Vehicle (Lotus Sutra’s teaching) leading to Buddhahood.68911

A poor, destitute son wanders for years and unknowingly ends up working for his wealthy father, who recognizes him but does not reveal their relationship. The father gradually elevates the son’s status, and eventually reveals the truth, allowing the son to inherit his wealth. The father is the Buddha, the son is ordinary beings, and the inheritance is Buddhahood. It teaches that all beings have Buddha-nature but may not recognize it until guided skillfully.138

Rain falls equally on all plants, but they absorb it according to their own capacities, growing into small, medium, or large herbs. The Buddha’s teachings are like the rain, nourishing all beings according to their abilities and readiness. Ultimately, all can attain Buddhahood, despite apparent differences in capacity.138

A guide leads a group seeking treasure through a dangerous wilderness. When they tire and want to turn back, the guide conjures a magical city for them to rest. Once they are refreshed, the city vanishes, and the guide urges them on to the true treasure. The guide is the Buddha, the travelers are beings, the phantom city is provisional teachings, and the treasure is ultimate enlightenment.138

A poor man visits a rich friend, gets drunk, and passes out. The friend sews a priceless jewel into the lining of the man’s garment without telling him. The man lives in poverty until, years later, his friend reveals the jewel. The rich friend is the Buddha, the poor man is sentient beings, and the jewel is Buddha-nature or the capacity for enlightenment, always present but unrecognized.138

A physician’s children mistakenly take poison and become ill. The physician (father) returns, prepares medicine, but some children are too deluded to take it. He pretends to die, and in their grief, the children finally take the medicine and recover. The physician is the Buddha, the children are beings, the poison is delusion, and the medicine is the Dharma. The parable teaches personal responsibility and the need to accept the Dharma to be saved.1710

A massive jeweled stupa rises from the earth, containing the body of the Buddha Prabhūtaratna, who appears to vouch for the truth of the Lotus Sutra. Shakyamuni Buddha and Prabhūtaratna sit side by side, and many Buddhas from other worlds gather. This parable symbolizes the universality and timelessness of the Lotus Sutra’s truth and the unity of all Buddhas.134
